en.extract:
The Nariño tapaculo (Scytalopus vicinior) is a species of bird in the family Rhinocryptidae. It is found in Colombia and Ecuador.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Nariño tapaculo was originally described as a subspecies of the Tacarcuna tapaculo (Scytalopus panamensis) and was retained there until after Krabbe and Schulenberg (1997) noted "no particular similarity, either in plumage or in voice, between true S. vicinior and S. panamensis". 


Description
The Nariño tapaculo looks similar to several other Andean tapaculos. The male's head is dark gray, the back is dark gray or brown, and the rump is cinnamon with dusky bars. The throat, chest, and belly are lighter gray and the flanks and vent area are yellowish brown with blackish bars. The female is similar, but the back is browner, the throat is lighter gray, and the lower belly sometimes yellower.


Distribution and habitat
The Nariño tapaculo is found on the west slope of the Andes from Colombia's Risaralda and Chocó Departments south to southwestern Cotopaxi Province in Ecuador. There it primarily inhabits the understory of moist montane forest and sometimes forest edges. In elevation it generally ranges from 1,250 to 2,000 m (4,100 to 6,560 ft) but can be found as high as 2,350 m (7,710 ft) in Ecuador.


Behavior
The Nariño tapaculo preys on a variety of insects, but little more is known about its feeding phenology. Little is also known about its breeding behavior other than that breeding condition specimens were collected in November, December, and April.
The Nariño tapaculo's song is "a fast series of well-enunciated, ringing notes that starts out with a brief stutter but then may go on for 15-30 seconds, pididi- [1].


Status
The IUCN has assessed the Nariño tapaculo as being of Least Concern. Despite its somewhat small range and unknown population numbers, both are believed large enough to warrant that classification.


References

The '''Nariño tapaculo''' ('''''Scytalopus vicinior''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Rhinocryptidae]]. It is found in [[Colombia]] and [[Ecuador]].<ref name=IOC11.1>{{cite web |url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/ |title=IOC World Bird List (v 11.1) |last1=Gill |first1= F. |last2=Donsker|first2=D.|last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. |date=January 2021 |access-date=January 14, 2021 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The Nariño tapaculo was originally described as a subspecies of the Tacarcuna tapaculo (''Scytalopus panamensis'') and was retained there until after Krabbe and Schulenberg (1997) noted "no particular similarity, either in plumage or in voice, between true ''S. vicinior'' and ''S. panamensis''".<ref>Krabbe, N., and T.S. Schulenberg. 1997. Species limits and natural history of ''Scytalopus'' tapaculos (Rhinocryptidae), with descriptions of the Ecuadorian taxa, including three new species. Pages 47-88 in J. V. Remsen, Jr. (editor), Studies in neotropical ornithology honoring Ted Parker. Ornithological Monographs number 48. American Ornithologists' Union, Washington, D.C.</ref> 

==Description==

The Nariño tapaculo looks similar to several other Andean tapaculos. The male's head is dark gray, the back is dark gray or brown, and the rump is cinnamon with dusky bars. The throat, chest, and belly are lighter gray and the flanks and vent area are yellowish brown with blackish bars. The female is similar, but the back is browner, the throat is lighter gray, and the lower belly sometimes yellower.<ref name=NATA-BOW>Schulenberg, T. S. and T. Johnson (2020). Nariño Tapaculo (''Scytalopus vicinior''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (T. S. Schulenberg, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.nartap2.01 retrieved April 28, 2021</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The Nariño tapaculo is found on the west slope of the [[Andes]] from Colombia's [[Risaralda Department|Risaralda]] and [[Chocó Department|Chocó]] Departments south to southwestern [[Cotopaxi Province]] in Ecuador. There it primarily inhabits the understory of moist [[montane forest]] and sometimes forest edges. In elevation it generally ranges from {{convert|1250|to|2000|m|ft|abbr=on}} but can be found as high as {{convert|2350|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Ecuador.<ref name=NATA-BOW/>

==Behavior==

The Nariño tapaculo preys on a variety of insects, but little more is known about its feeding [[phenology]]. Little is also known about its breeding behavior other than that breeding condition specimens were collected in November, December, and April.<ref name=NATA-BOW/>

The Nariño tapaculo's song is "a fast series of well-enunciated, ringing notes that starts out with a brief stutter but then may go on for 15-30 seconds, pididi- [https://www.xeno-canto.org/248313].<ref>{{cite book | last=Ridgely | first=R.S. | last2=Greenfield | first2=P.J. | title=The Birds of Ecuador | publisher=Cornell University Press | volume=2 |  date=2001 | page=451 | isbn=978-0-8014-8721-7 }}</ref>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the Nariño tapaculo as being of Least Concern. Despite its somewhat small range and unknown population numbers, both are believed large enough to warrant that classification.<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" />
